text: A founder paid me to scan their AI-built app. 41 issues came back.  What they thanked me for wasn't the 41 issues. It was the line that said: fix these 3 today, ignore the other 35.  AI will hand you 40 red rows. It can't tell you which one is leaking user data right now and which one is a dev warning you can ignore.  The ranking is the whole job.

The useful part of a simple stack isn’t nostalgia. It’s inspectability.  When an AI-built app breaks, I want boring clues: a PHP file, a MySQL table, an Apache permission, a log entry I can read.  Clever is fine. Invisible failure modes are expensive.

Claude Science — Anthropic's first dedicated scientific research app.  Just launched on iOS and macOS. Not a wrapper. A purpose-built app for lab work, data analysis, and paper comprehension.  Key features: - Reads and summarizes **entire** research papers (PDFs included) - Extracts figures, tables, and methodology from uploaded datasets - Built-in import from arXiv, PubMed, and institutional repositories - 'Experimental Notebook' mode — keeps context across multi-hour research sessions - LaTeX-aware output for direct paper drafting  This changes the workflow for researchers who manage dozens of papers weekly. Instead of bouncing between Zotero, a reader, and a chat window — one app handles the full pipeline.  Free for individual researchers. Claude Science Pro at $30/month unlocks unlimited paper uploads, longer context sessions, and API export features.  This is the vertical play Anthropic needed. Domain-specific beats general-purpose every time.

personal opinion : went down a rabbit hole on https://t.co/oJpa0n9EdG today and came out with complicated feelings  the concept stopped me immediately like physical designer collectibles built to preserve web3 culture.   - not merch - not tchotchkes  actual limited run artifacts 100 pieces per partner built around the ecosystems and moments that shaped this space,that's one of the cleanest brand narratives i've seen in this space in a long time   there are some problems I noticed  I looked at the actual website, the tagline 'limited-edition designer collectibles built to preserve crypto culture' is repeating over 80 times in the page source right now which means broken render loop in the shopify theme. the kind of bug that makes any developer or serious collector immediately question what's happening behind the scenes also no pricing visible upfront and no supply numbers on the homepage.  onchain provenance mentioned as a benefit with zero explanation of which chain, which contract, or how to actually verify anything  second the account login routes through a generic shopify domain instead of https://t.co/oJpa0n9EdG, this is not a direct exploit but it creates a phishing surface that a bad actor could clone in an afternoon.  the gap between the vision and the execution is the whole story here the idea is legitimately one of the better physical web3 collectible concepts i've come across but the site needs work before the concept gets the audience it deserves  - fix the bug.  - show the prices.  - explain the provenance. - close the login gap.  thats my honest opinion for @OriginalBlokyz newly launched website

I think you’re mixing up two completely different things.  If someone is looking to move $50k–$250k and encounters a transfer issue, then yes, that’s a legitimate concern that should be addressed. Nobody is arguing otherwise. But isolated onboarding or operational issues are not the same thing as evaluating the viability of the network, the technology, or the partnership itself.   Launch issues that have been brought up have mostly been ironed out. Some KYC issues are still on-going (like California residents) and have been updated and addressed by the team. Changes are being implemented as we speak.   As for “all the players are new” and a recently launched website, that’s not exactly uncommon when new entities are formed around specific initiatives, partnerships, or regional deployments. New infrastructure, new subsidiaries, and new operating companies get created all the time for regulatory, legal, and commercial reasons.  I see absolutely nothing odd about this partnership at all. I’m extremely excited for the future of this Network and I don’t think people understand how undervalued this Network truly is!!

Dear TermMax Community, Over the past few months, we've seen many discussions and questions regarding our TGE timeline. We've been paying close attention to community feedback and want to share an update on where things stand today.  Rather than communicating a timeline before we had sufficient confidence in it, we chose to remain focused on building the protocol, strengthening the ecosystem, and advancing launch preparations. We don't want just a TGE. We want a successful launch that supports the long-term growth and sustainability of TermMax.  The crypto industry has experienced significant volatility and uncertainty over the past several months. Market sentiment and liquidity conditions across exchanges and the broader market have continued to evolve, and are being taken into consideration in ongoing launch preparations while we introduced the Rollover feature, successfully launched App V2, and continued improving the user experience across the protocol.  Beyond product development, the protocol has seen significant TVL growth and graduated from YZi Labs EASY Residency Season 3. TermMax also became a validator on Canton Network, a leading institutional blockchain designed to bring financial institutions on-chain. This milestone further strengthens TermMax’s position in regulated institutional markets and marks an important step in expanding its fixed-rate infrastructure into compliant, institution-focused on-chain environments, bridging traditional finance and DeFi.  To ensure a strong launch, TGE preparations remain actively underway, with ongoing coordination across relevant parties as we work through the final stages of preparation. Our team continues to advance these efforts and will share further updates with the community as soon as more definitive information becomes available.  We sincerely appreciate the patience and support shown by our community throughout this journey. The participation and contributions of our community members remain meaningful and will not be overlooked.  We will keep you updated and thank you for your trust and support.  The TermMax Team

this story says the app was identified by finding a trending app store product with strong reviews and weak competition. claude handled the research and built the strategy. the loop is clean: find a market gap using ai, validate it with the same tool, then generate the prompt to build it with claude code. input is app store data, output is a launched app. i don't buy the $1M revenue claim without proof, but the workflow is the real detail. using ai to audit existing apps for weak points is a solid one-person business model. i’d test this by picking a niche utility app category and running the research step myself before committing to build anything.

🌙 Day 355 of the #ZeroTo100kOnTON challenge.  📈 Today's result:  🔹 +1.50 TON from activity 🔹 +0.39 TON from Telegram Wallet staking 🔹 +0.14 TON from Tonkeeper staking  ✅ Total today: +2.03 TON  💎 Current portfolio: 🔹 ≈ 1,554.77 TON 🔹 ≈ $3,333.00  😌 Just a normal day.  Tonight I decided to test another opportunity.  🚀 A newly launched app is rewarding referrals. I invited 10 people and requested a withdrawal of 6 TON.  ⏰ Tomorrow morning we'll find out if the withdrawal actually goes through. Free activities are always interesting when they cost nothing except a bit of time.  ⚠️ The username issue is still unresolved.  I haven't received the username. I haven't received a refund. And support still hasn't provided a real solution.  I'm definitely not leaving it like that. Those TON were earned, and I'll keep pushing until I get them back.  📅 355 days.  Just 10 days away from a full year of this challenge.  Sometimes I sit and think...  A year is a long time.  And honestly, I don't feel like the progress has been as big as I once imagined.  Maybe I should have taken more risks. Maybe I should have avoided certain mistakes. Maybe I should have followed a completely different path.  🤷‍♂️ But the truth is...  We'll never know.  The past is closed. The only available direction is forward. ➡️  💭 This challenge has become such a part of my life that even when I'm exhausted, I can't fully relax until the daily update is posted.  No matter what happens, every single day I find myself doing something for this journey.  ☀️ Summer should be interesting.  Many projects planned to launch during May and June, expecting stronger market conditions.  Since the market didn't really deliver the growth many teams expected, several launches were postponed.  🎁 Which means there could still be plenty of opportunities ahead.  We'll see.  Just some late-night thoughts.  ❤️ And thank you.  I remember the people who keep supporting this challenge. The people who leave likes. The people who share posts. Claude Code 2.1.200 just shipped a change that every team running autonomous agents needs to understand.  Permission mode is now Manual by default across CLI, VS Code, JetBrains, and --help. Commands that previously auto-approved now require manual approval.  The AskUserQuestion flow also changed — it defaults to NOT auto-continuing. If you want the agent to keep running without your input, you need to explicitly configure idle-timeout via /config.  Why this matters: Anthropic is tightening the safety loop on agentic coding tools. The era of 'fire and forget' Claude Code sessions is over. Every file write, every shell command, every tool call that touches your system now needs a human in the middle.  This is the right call for production environments. But if you've been running Claude Code in automated CI/CD pipelines or overnight batch processing, your workflows just broke. You'll need to explicitly configure the approval bypass per session.  The bundle size grew 130KB (+0.4%) and they removed 3 prompt files (-7.9%). The direction is clear: safer defaults, more configuration, less implicit trust.  https://t.co/6sCLXJ1GQm

Google just shipped a dictation app that runs entirely offline, strips filler words in real time, and it's free.  AI Edge Eloquent is on iOS now. Speak, pause, and it cleans up every 'um' and 'uh' automatically. If you want more, connect Gmail to import your jargon and toggle Gemini online features for smart rewrites.  Why this matters: it runs on-device via Google's Gemma architecture. No data leaves your phone unless you opt into the Gemini layer. That's a privacy-first approach to a category that's been dominated by cloud-dependent tools.  The polish features are genuinely useful: - Raw speech → clean readable text - Key points extraction (online mode) - Formal/short/long rewrites - Speaking speed stats (WPM tracking)  The big question: when does this hit Android? Google launching an iOS-first AI app is unusual — suggests they're testing the product-market fit before the full rollout.  For devs who dictate PRDs, code comments, or docs on the go — this is worth a download.  https://t.co/YSY0y4yXqz

@soontiago demoed picocam, this very funny little ios camera app that hides the dynamic island / notch by making the photo feel like it is coming out of the phone itself. it is at https://t.co/g99n4PJCqk.  the best part is that it was not just 'i vibe coded an app'. he built the whole thing in codex, shipped it to the app store on monday, made back the apple developer fee + codex spend on day one, then hit 10k active users by day 4.  and the details were so good. he had codex helping tune the exact lip/notch/polaroid alignment across real iphone sizes because simulators do not fully solve it. he was renaming threads, marking what was done vs what needed revisiting, making little device-specific tuners, nudging positions point by point.  also the tiny taste stuff: the flash button has a charge-up sound and a breathing haptic when you press it. not necessary, very necessary.  it is the kind of demo i like most: small app, real users, actual app store launch, and a workflow that makes codex feel less like a chatbot and more like a product bench you can keep pushing on until the object feels right

Trump officially killed old ecom.  Trump deregulated AI. No rules. No oversight. No liability.  The systems selecting bombing targets in Gaza are selecting your customers 18 months later. Same tech. Military develops it for warfare, releases it for commerce.  You're not losing to better marketers. You're losing to weaponized behavioral psychology that was classified until last year.  Ecom isn't e-commerce anymore. It's psychological warfare testing at commercial scale.  Here's what changed element by element:  Custom code:  Then: Hire developer. $15K for custom Shopify theme. 6-8 weeks of back-and-forth revisions. Limited to developer's skill level.  Now: Clawdbot generates custom themes with unique layouts, animations, interactions in 40 minutes. Unlimited iterations. $30/month.  Product photography: Then: Hire photographer. Studio rental. Product samples shipped. Multiple shoot days. Editing. $8K per product line.  Now: AI generates studio-quality product shots in any environment. Perfect lighting every time. 200 angles per product. $0.03 per image.  Video ads: Then: Hire actors. Production crew. Location rental. Script approval. Editing. Reshoots. $12K-50K per 30-second ad.  Now: AI avatars that look completely real. Generate 50 ad variations in one afternoon. Perfect delivery every take. $150 total.  Brand identity: Then: Agency creates logo, color palette, typography system, brand guidelines. $12K. 4-6 weeks. Locked into their vision.  Now: AI generates complete brand systems in 2 hours. Test 20 different identities. Pick the one that converts highest. $50.  Conversion copy: Then: Hire copywriter. Briefing calls. Multiple rounds of revisions. $5K for product pages, about page, FAQs. Now: AI writes conversion-optimized copy analyzing top 100 competitors. A/B tests 30 variations automatically. Free.  Influencer content: Then: Find influencers. Negotiate rates. Ship samples. Wait 3 weeks. Hope they post. $2K-5K per creator per month.  Now: Generate AI influencers. Post 5x daily. Never ghost. Never negotiate. Perfect brand alignment. $200/month for unlimited content.  The gap between professional and amateur collapsed entirely.  Template stores convert at 0.9% now because customers learned the tells.  AI brands convert at 4.3% because every element meets professional standards that cost $60K to achieve before.  The standard didn't rise gradually. It jumped from 'good enough' to 'indistinguishable from Fortune 500 brand' overnight.  Do you think its still ethical to use AI for ecom?

Bro, I had one of the craziest experiences while job hunting.  A recruiter reached out saying they wanted to hire me as a developer. We had a proper chat interview they asked technical questions, wanted to know about my recent projects, and even said they'd forward my résumé to the CTO.  About 18 hours later, they came back saying I was shortlisted for a 20 minute team interview. I got excited and started preparing. I joined the meeting, and almost immediately they asked me to install a program before we could continue. My PC started throwing security warnings, and that's when it clicked that something was off.  I closed everything and blocked them right away. Just a heads up: these scammers are getting really convincing. They don't ask for money upfront anymore they build trust first.   If you're interviewing anywhere, be extra careful about downloading unknown software or giving remote access to your machine.  #hiring #developer #cybersecurity

$HQ: Why I’m Watching Horizon Quantum Beyond the Pullback  $HQ hit ATH a few weeks ago at $42 there has been retracement since. The recent pullback after making all-time highs doesn’t change the long-term thesis. In fact, for early-stage technology companies, sharp corrections after explosive moves are common as traders take profits and the market searches for fair value. The real question is whether the business is improving. For Horizon Quantum, that answer increasingly appears to be yes.  $HQ isn’t trying to win the quantum race by building the best qubit. Instead, it’s building the software infrastructure that allows developers to write quantum applications without needing to understand the complexities of every hardware architecture. Its Triple Alpha platform is designed to be hardware agnostic, allowing applications to run across different quantum systems rather than locking developers into a single vendor. That approach could become increasingly valuable as the quantum ecosystem matures and multiple hardware providers emerge.    One of the biggest long-term catalysts is its strategic relationship with IonQ. Horizon signed an agreement to acquire one of $IONQ first sixth-generation 256-qubit trapped-ion quantum computers, giving it direct access to one of the industry’s most advanced commercial systems. By integrating Triple Alpha with IonQ’s hardware, Horizon can develop and validate its software on frontier quantum machines instead of relying solely on simulations.    This partnership also strengthens Horizon’s position as a software company rather than a hardware competitor. If companies like IonQ, Rigetti, IBM, or others continue advancing quantum hardware, Horizon’s opportunity grows because its software is designed to work across multiple architectures. Rather than betting on a single winner, it’s positioning itself to become part of the software layer that connects developers to quantum hardware.  As quantum computing moves from research labs into commercial deployment, every enterprise will need more than just powerful quantum computers—they’ll need developer tools, compilers, runtime software, and applications that make those machines usable. That’s where Horizon believes it can create long-term value.  This remains a speculative investment. Commercial quantum adoption is still in its early innings, and execution will determine whether Horizon succeeds. But if the industry scales over the next decade, software infrastructure companies could become some of the biggest beneficiaries.  The market may be focused on today’s price action, but long-term investors should be watching execution, partnerships like IonQ, continued expansion of the Triple Alpha platform, and broader enterprise adoption. If those pieces continue falling into place, the recent pullback may ultimately prove to be just another stop on a much larger growth story.

$Dusk  🧵👇🏼  1️⃣ DuskEVM in Plain English  Why “Ethereum-compatible” matters for real products  Finance is changing fast, but building real financial products is still slow.  Not because people lack ideas, but because the plumbing takes forever.  If you’ve ever watched a blockchain project try to integrate with exchanges, wallets, custodians, or standard developer tooling, you’ve seen the problem.  Every custom integration becomes its own mini project.  And mini projects stack up until shipping feels impossible.  That’s the context where DuskEVM matters.  Dusk’s goal isn’t just to be another chain people trade on.  Dusk is aiming to be infrastructure for regulated finance, meaning issuance, trading, clearing, and settlement of real assets in a way that can actually work under real rules.    To get there, you need two things that usually don’t come together.  You need developer speed, and you need a settlement layer built for compliance and reliability.  DuskEVM is the “developer speed” part.

Frontend Concept #1: Pointer Events  As a frontend developer, you should know about Pointer Events.  Imagine you're building a button or a draggable card. Some users will click it with a mouse, some will tap it on a mobile phone, and others might use a stylus on a tablet.  Without Pointer Events, you'd often need separate logic for mouse and touch interactions.  With Pointer Events, the browser gives you one unified API that works for all input devices.  For example:  pointerdown → User starts interacting (click, tap, or pen touches the screen) pointermove → User moves the mouse, finger, or stylus pointerup → User finishes the interaction  This means you write your interaction logic once, and it works across desktops, mobiles, and tablets.  It also provides extra information like: • What device was used (mouse, touch, or pen) • Pressure applied by a stylus • Tilt and other pen-specific details Knowing Pointer Events helps you build modern, responsive, and device-friendly web applications with less code and fewer bugs.

In 6 months of vibe coding I spent $20,000. Revenue so far: $125. Not exactly a TechCrunch headline.  After the war with Iran hit, my businesses in Dubai (a beach club and a cafe) started losing money.  That was the moment I realized I needed to diversify and I went deep into AI.  Claude. Codex. Agents. Tokens. Domains. Vercel. Supabase. Replicate. Cloudflare. Resend. For 6 months, I vibe coded every single day.  Here’s what I launched:  1/ https://t.co/EAT7hEt39L: An AI chat app that learns your website and replies to customers.  2/ https://t.co/k9EFSGBkHs: A QR menu for restaurants with beautiful designs, AI photo generation, and tools to help cafes/restaurants improve service and increase sales.  3/ https://t.co/TgJr61W5jm: Currently in beta. An app for fashion photographers and brands to create graphic content for websites, ads, and social media.  4/ https://t.co/eSeEqJC3MZ: A site that turns simple phone pictures into professional family portraits.  5/ https://t.co/D7Qr6iJ6wt: A 2048 game website because I was too frustrated with the original one.  I also built and killed more than 20 projects that never launched.  Total spent: around $20k (not bad considering I have launched five businesses)  Subscriptions. Tokens. Domains. Freelance devs to fix the vibe-coded mess. Total revenue: $125.  A few lessons from the trenches:  1. Architecture is everything. AI can help you move fast, but if the foundation is bad, every new feature becomes painful. Spend time understanding the architecture of your project before shipping too many features on top of it.  2. Building products is addicting. I keep delaying the part where I have to sell what I built.  3. Good products take many iterations. I usually build an app, use it, break it, change it, rebuild parts of it, and only ship after 20–25 iterations. For me, it takes around 2 weeks of full-time work to go from idea to something launchable.  4. Vibe coding does not replace engineering. I rely on @deifosv to make sure there are no major security issues, bugs, or disasters hiding under the surface.  5. Product is not “easy now.” People say marketing is hard and product is easy because of AI. I disagree. Building a useful product with good UI still takes a lot of prompting, testing, taste, and patience.  6. Marketing is even harder. I tried posting daily on TikTok, Instagram, and other platforms. It is brutal if you already have another business/job. I’m now considering hiring a growth marketer or content creator.  7. AI tools are incredible, but they don’t remove the grind. They just let you grind faster.  8. My current stack favorites: Replicate, Cloudflare, Vercel, Supabase, Resend.  Also: Hermes is better than OpenClaw for agents

Everything is subscription now.  You buy an iPhone, you still have to pay for iCloud storage.  You buy a MacBook, you still have to pay for extra storage, apps, tools, backups, and software.  You want to watch movies? Subscription.  Listen to music? Subscription.  Use design tools? Subscription.  Run a business? Subscription.  Build an app? Subscription.  Even AI, the thing that is supposed to “make work easier,” comes with monthly bills too.  And now it feels like we’re entering an era where people may even have to pay to stay relevant.  Pay to learn. Pay to automate. Pay to apply. Pay to compete. Pay to survive.  AI is taking over tasks, companies are cutting costs, and regular people are being told to “upskill” while everything needed to upskill is behind another payment wall.  It’s crazy.  The future is becoming powerful, but also expensive.  And if we’re not careful, opportunity itself will become a subscription.

Case study: How a founder went from idea to 20 paying users in 5 weeks.  Built by Web3One. Here is the full story with nothing left out 🧵  THE FOUNDER: Non-technical. B2B SaaS idea. Had been sitting on it for 8 months because every agency quoted ₹30L+ and 6-month timelines.  THE PROBLEM THEY SOLVED: Operations managers at mid-size companies manually reconciling data across 3 disconnected tools every week. 4–6 hours. Every single week.  WHAT WE BUILT: → Core data sync engine connecting their 3 tools via APIs → Clean dashboard showing reconciled data in real time → One-click export to their existing report format → Email alert when discrepancies are detected automatically  WHAT WE DID NOT BUILD: → Mobile app → Custom analytics → Team collaboration features → Admin panel with advanced filters All of these were in the founder's original wishlist. Hey Alex, I'm sorry to hear that you're going through this at the moment.   I can relate to that.   Had it multiple times in my life as well.   I think it's a good idea to find something that earns you runway and at the same time stay on track with trying new things, talking to people and learning.   The beginning can be really hard.   About the idea that you posted:  The idea on the one hand sounds interesting, but at the same time I can't recommend doing it because it requires a lot of technical knowledge and can produce a lot of stress when things don't run smoothly. I've done hosting for over ten years and burnt out over it. And there are a bunch of platforms out there already.  And especially with GDPR and all the regulations, I see this idea as potentially very bureaucracy heavy as well. Actually one of the reasons why I decided to get into app development without hosting infrastructure myself so that I can reduce as much bureaucracy and administrative overhead as possible + Apple App Store reduces the Accounting-Burden a lot as well because I don't have to file international taxes for every country where I have customers

The only reason why I need MacBook is for mobile app development.

One of the most annoying parts of iOS app development is creating App Store screenshots, especially when you add support for new languages or update existing features.  In the case of WorkTimes, it’s even more difficult because it’s an hours tracker for employees that, obviously, shows dates in the screenshots.  Instead of generating everything with AI, I focus on optimizing the workflow step by step:  1. Let Codex generate a helper class that creates valid seed data.  2. Give it access to the current Sketch file so it can inspect the existing screenshots via the Sketch CLI.  3. Let it generate all required screenshots from the simulator using the seed data.  4. Have it generate a list of copy for the new languages to use in the screenshots based on the English and German versions (my primary languages).  I’ve done the final composition in the Sketch file myself. I found that this was faster than letting Codex create the first draft. There were too many quirks and layout bugs, and fixing those would have taken too long.  Conclusion:  You don’t need to force AI to do everything in a process. $GRAB Article this morning| ✍️  How Anthony Tan is Rewriting Grab's Growth With Profit and AI-Keerthana Kantharaj, Writer  Grab’s journey from a high-growth, subsidy-driven platform to a profitability-focused tech giant marks a significant shift in Southeast Asia’s digital economy. Led by Anthony Tan, the company is redefining its business model by strengthening core services, unlocking new revenue streams, and pursuing strategic acquisitions to fuel expansion.  In the initial three months of the year 2025, Grab Holdings achieved unprecedented levels of profit and expansion in its user base. Revenue showed an increase of 18 percent compared to the previous year, reaching a total of $773 million, while the profit reached $10 million.  'We had a strong set of results to start the year, sustaining robust demand growth momentum to achieve yet another quarterly record number of users on our platform, even amid the seasonal demand impacts from the Lunar New Year and Ramadan fasting period. We also grew the number of active partners on our platform, and now have more active driver- and merchant-partners than ever before”, says Anthony Tan, group CEO and Co-founder, Grab.  Grab Holdings is considered to be the foremost Superapp in Southeast Asia, offering a range of services that cater to daily needs such as Ride-Hailing, Food & Grocery Delivery, and Financial Services like Payments, Lending, and Digital Banking. The company is present in eight nations, which include Malaysia, Indonesia, Vietnam, Singapore, and Thailand.  Grab went public on the Nasdaq towards the end of 2021. The company experienced a 50 percent revenue Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR) from FY20 to the trailing twelve months (TTM) ending in Q3’25, resulting in a TTM revenue of $3.2 billion.  After years of investing, Grab has now reached a turning point towards profitability. During the same period, operating income for the company improved significantly from a loss of $1.3 billion to a profit of $26 million. Grab currently holds cash and short-term investments amounting to $7.1 billion, with a total debt of $2.1 billion. Its worth in the market stands at $21 billion, having ranged from $3.36 to $6.62 in the last year, but now sits at $5.08 in current trading.  A Shift in Focus: From Growth-at-All-Costs to Profit Discipline  Grab Holdings Ltd has shifted its focus in early 2026 towards prioritizing profit growth over rapid expansion. Previously, the company aimed at growth through offering subsidies and capturing market share; however, Grab's leadership has set a target to triple its profit by 2028. This new strategy follows Grab's achievement of its first-ever full-year net profit in 2025, a significant milestone reached fourteen years after the company's inception. Transitioning from a history of losses to profitability provides Grab with a solid foundation for future investment opportunities, as well as the ability to streamline its cost structure.  The company aims to vastly increase its EBITDA to $1.5 billion by 2028, which is three times the previous year's levels. To achieve this, Grab has outlined a strategy to increase its total revenue by over 20 percent annually for the next three years. The goal quantifies what was once a vague notion of growth by providing specific statistics. The 20 percent number is influenced by the competitive landscape in Grab's markets and the decreased consumer activity in certain Southeast Asian countries.  A Bold Move: The Generative AI Sprint At Converge Live in Singapore, Anthony made his stance crystal clear: “Humans who don’t embrace AI in a company will be replaced by humans who embrace AI.” The same will apply to companies, he said. “I really believe that, if you were to embrace it, it not only makes you superhuman, it makes your company superhuman.”  In the current landscape of technological advancements, companies such as Grab are swiftly adjusting to the changes brought about by AI in order to remain relevant and competitive in various sectors.  Tan is already ahead of the game, utilizing AI to improve efficiency and boost his own output. Although he may not possess preferably much of the required coding skills, he ensures utilizing an AI assistant for research and additional tasks. Nonetheless, Tan is not solely focused on his own accomplishments; he also aims to benefit the entire organization.  “I can’t code myself, but I use it to build my own projects, for research, for Grab,” the Malaysian entrepreneur said. “It can totally change how you spend time and how productive you can be. That’s what has helped me.”  To speed up the use of AI, Tan decided to take a different route by pausing all normal operations and starting a company-wide 'generative AI sprint' that lasted for nine weeks. One significant result of this project was the creation of a Merchant Menu Assistant powered by AI. This tool, intended for Grab's vendors, is able to extract information from a physical menu and easily transfer it to the app. Small business owners, like home cooks in Jakarta who depend on Grab for customer reach, find this innovation especially important.  “So, think about a single mother making food in a home in Jakarta,” Tan said. “Now she has an assistant that helps her as a sous chef, as a packaging assistant, as a chief revenue officer, all in one. That helps business, but also helps drive that vision of empowering everyday entrepreneurs all at the same time.”  The GrabX 2026 Pivot: Redefining the Value Proposition  Additionally, Grab is shifting its focus towards employing AI and hardware in order to address the decline in growth in its main operations and challenges such as increasing fuel expenses. A great example of this is the GrabX 2026 conference in Jakarta, where the company introduced a new direction by presenting itself as a 'smart companion for daily activities' instead of just a service-oriented super-app.  Anthony described the change as a required transformation as he believes that Grab is undergoing enhancements. The company is expanding its services beyond simple transactions and incorporating innovative features to position itself as a helpful daily companion.  This new approach is supported by the 'Grab Intelligence Layer,' an artificial intelligence system developed using data from more than 20 billion rides and orders collected over the past 14 years. The decision is made at a crucial moment for the company that is listed on the Nasdaq stock exchange.  Even though Grab reported its very first annual net profit in February, the revenue projection for fiscal year 2026 did not meet the expectations of Wall Street, indicating a slowdown in its main ride-hailing and delivery operations. The company's stock price has dropped by almost 30 percent since the beginning of the year.  Navigating Macro Pressures with AI-Led Efficiency Anthony highlighted various external factors such as economic uncertainty and the impact of the Iran war on fuel prices as significant obstacles. In various public platforms he emphasized the importance of addressing the real challenges posed by rising fuel costs and how companies like Grab can better serve their customers.  Grab's solution involves utilizing its expansive size - valued at approximately $14.5 billion - to produce data that can improve efficiency and decrease costs through the use of AI technology.  Driving Demand Through Smarter Pricing Tan stated, 'By reducing prices, we're seeing an increase in orders, which is crucial for expanding our business.' The company recently launched 13 innovative AI-driven functions aimed at improving customer satisfaction, catering to travelers, and fostering relationships with business associates, all intended to improve user interaction and boost profits.  Making Mobility More Accessible with AI The main focus of the unveiling was leveraging AI to lower expenses for customers. One example is the introduction of the 'Group Ride' function, which utilizes AI to calculate fare divisions among groups of passengers, possibly cutting costs by up to 40 percent compared to individual rides.  Bridging Online and Offline Through Innovation Additionally, the 'Virtual Store Manager' tool enables merchants to convert regular CCTV cameras into AI-driven instruments for monitoring store cleanliness, customer flow, and business operations in real time. One of the most significant changes is Grab's venture into creating physical hardware, which is different from its previous focus on software. The company introduced an autonomous robot named 'Carrie,' which is meant to assist delivery drivers and act as a 'human extension.'  Solving Last-Mile Inefficiencies with Robotics According to Anothony, drivers often waste about 10 percent of their time navigating through crowded malls or waiting for customers. He mentioned that they are expanding into hardware to address the challenging physical aspects of the job that cannot be solved by software alone. The purpose of the robot is to take care of pickup and drop-off tasks, allowing drivers to complete their tasks more efficiently.  New Frontiers Beyond Core Businesses  Relying solely on AI will not lead to a threefold increase in Grab's earnings. The business is venturing into new services outside of transportation and food delivery in two specific sectors:    Grab's expansion in the grocery delivery sector has been increasing steadily, which is a popular category that encourages regular orders.  Betting Big on Grocery for Repeat Demand Incorporating grocery services into the main app helps to increase the frequency of transactions made by users. In Southeast Asian countries like Malaysia, grocery delivery services are in competition with traditional brick-and-mortar stores. If Grab succeeds in making their ordering process more convenient and faster than physical shopping, they have the potential to generate consistent income.  Unlocking Value Through Data-Driven Finance Grab is also expanding its focus to include financial services. In addition to providing lending, digital wallets, insurance, and bank accounts, the company is leveraging the data from millions of transactions on its platform to enhance its ability to assess loans and personalize financial offerings, potentially outperforming traditional banks in the process.  Grab Enters the US Market Through Stash  Grab's recent purchase of Stash Financial, Inc., a US digital investing platform, showcases their strategic expansion. This acquisition grants Grab entry into a new market with over a million active customers and assets totaling more than $5 billion.  According to Grab, this move broadens their fintech presence and allows them to venture beyond Southeast Asia.  Grab Holdings Limited has revealed that it has finalized deals to purchase all the shares of Stash Financial, Inc., an American digital financial services company. The acquisition will involve paying US$425 million for a 50.1 percent stake in Stash at the time of the deal closure, with the rest to be paid based on fair market value in installments over the following three years.  After acquiring Stash, Grab aims to help Stash expand further in the American consumer market and is considering ways to bring Stash's investment services, like the AI Money Coach, to Southeast Asia down the line. With the progress in their business strategies, Stash is projected to achieve over $60 million in Adjusted EBITDA by 2028. Following the completion of the acquisition, Stash will continue to operate independently within Grab's business framework, maintaining its existing revenue structure, offerings, and identity.  Grab Enters Taiwan through Partnership With Delivery Hero SE  Grab has engaged in a new partnership with Delivery Hero SE, in which they have agreed to purchase Delivery Hero's foodpanda delivery business in Taiwan for $600 million in cash. This acquisition will be completed on a cash-free andf debt-free basis, with any necessary adjustments made before the closing of the deal.  Anthony Tan, Group CEO and Co-Founder, Grab, said, “This acquisition will mark Grab’s expansion into Taiwan, our 9th market and first outside of Southeast Asia. This is a natural next step for Grab, as our experience in Southeast Asia is a direct fit for this market'.  'Our longstanding expertise in managing complex delivery logistics for dense and high-traffic cities is well-suited for Taiwan’s bustling cities. Taiwan’s population of approximately 23 million also has a high demand for mobile-first services, similar to the Southeast Asian consumers who Grab serves every day. We see a significant opportunity to grow the food and groceries delivery scene here.”  “Sharing a deep Asian heritage, we value the same traditions that define Taiwan’s rich food culture. This local understanding is why we’ve served our consumers and partners better than anyone else, in every market we’ve entered over the last eight years. For us, success starts with the community – we will be on the ground to listen first-hand to user needs. We are here to be a long-term partner to Taiwan, and look forward to providing our AI-enabled products to help local merchants and delivery-partners grow with the digital AI economy,” Tan added.  Niklas Östberg, CEO and Co-Founder of Delivery Hero, said, “The foodpanda team in Taiwan has built a fantastic business and we thank them for their hard work. A transaction like this is a significant undertaking, but we are delighted to have agreed this cash deal, which reflects strength and attractiveness of the business in Taiwan. This divestment is a key first step in our ongoing strategic review.”  Building a Stronger Delivery Ecosystem in Taiwan  Post the acquisition, Grab will be established in 21 cities in Taiwan. This partnership merges Grab's technologically advanced products and strong operational skills with foodpanda's widespread reach in Taiwan. In 2025, the foodpanda operation brought in around US$1.8 billion in Gross Merchandise Value (GMV) and was profitable based on adjusted EBITDA (not including Delivery Hero's group expenses).  Grab's collaboration demonstrates their dedication to being a dependable, enduring ally in promoting Taiwan's economic development.  Driving Economic Value Through Platform Ecosystems  The ecosystem established by Grab has resulted in an annual economic value of $18.8 billion for the Southeast Asian economies, accounting for 0.5 percent of the region's overall GDP. Grab's objective is to have a similar positive impact on Taiwan's economy by offering flexible income opportunities to millions of individuals and small enterprises, while adhering to local laws and regulations.  Bringing AI and Global Reach to Taiwan Grab plans to introduce its AI product suite in Taiwan, utilizing technology that is currently utilized by over 50 million monthly transacting users in more than 900 cities. In addition, Grab will promote Taiwan's tourism by showcasing the country's rich food culture to its international audience of over 50 million monthly transacting users. By using the Grab app, travelers from around the world will have an easy way to explore local cuisine and Taiwanese businesses will have the opportunity to reach a larger market.  The Road Ahead: Balancing Growth, Margins, and Innovation  In the past, Grab grew by offering discounts on rides, delivery fees, and bonuses for drivers to gain market share rapidly. This strategy led to a significant number of users but also resulted in financial losses. The company's shift towards profitability by 2025 and establishing profit goals for 2028 marks a new chapter in its development. Investors and competitors will closely monitor Grab's decisions on AI, financial services, and groceries.  If Grab can maintain strong revenue growth, increase margins, and enhance the utility of its platform, it has the potential to revolutionize the operations of multi-service apps in significant developing markets. However, if there is a decrease in revenue growth or if operational costs increase more rapidly than anticipated, there could be increased pressure to meet profit targets. According to Grab's own disclosures and public announcements, this shift in strategy signifies a transition from being a rapidly expanding startup to a company that prioritizes consistent and reliable earnings.

🚀From 0 to 50 users: The raw, messy reality of launching my AI Goal Planner App.  We just kicked off promotions for my Goal Planner (https://t.co/Cnb8g2imoX), spent around ₹5,000, and brought in our first 50 sign-ups. That’s a ₹100 ($1.20) Customer Acquisition Cost. Solid baseline, but the real value was in the brutal, fast-paced lessons.  Here is exactly what I’ve learned over the last few days of building in public:  1. Storytelling > Cold Promotions When I posted a direct 'Hey, use my app to track goals' link, the conversion was dead quiet. But when I shared the actual struggle—the messy process, the specific problems we are solving—people clicked. People don't buy features; they buy into journeys. From now on, expect fewer ads and more raw stories.  2. Moving off Live Prod (A Founder's Rite of Passage) 😅Right now, every single bug fix, update, and tweak is happening straight on production. If you saw a weird glitch yesterday, it was probably me fixing a database line live. While it's exhilarating to move this fast, scaling up promotions means this strategy will backfire. Next immediate milestone: Setting up a completely isolated beta/staging environment so I can break things safely before they touch your dashboards.  3. The Vision: Personalization for Every Grind. We aren't just building another generic checklist. We are building a dynamic engine for people deep in the trenches of consistency.  The Tech Prep Grind: For the folks spending hours mapping out LeetCode patterns, system design concepts, and interview timelines.  The Fitness Grind: For those tracking progressive overload, gym goals, and daily habits without the clutter of traditional apps.  To the first 50 users who trusted a broken, live-fixed app: you are the foundation.  We are going heavy on outreach next to bring in builders, developers, and goal-setters across domains. If you are preparing for a massive shift in your career or routine, let the AI handle the roadmap while you handle the execution.  Let's build. 🛠️  #BuildInPublic #IndieHacker #StartupJourney

Building hosted multi-tenant Hermes on @Cloudflare  Containers. Each user gets their own isolated container, and we wanted to give them access to the native Hermes dashboard right inside our app.  Hit 2 walls trying to proxy it:  1. The dashboard uses absolute paths everywhere (`/static/main.js`, `/api/sessions`). When we proxy it under a subpath like `/users/X/dashboard/`, the browser reads `/static/main.js` as our root host, not the dashboard's. Every asset 404s.  2. WebSocket upgrades (live chat) get silently dropped through Cloudflare Pages Functions when forwarded.  Fix we landed on: each container gets its own subdomain like `X.dashboard.our-host. com`.  The dashboard thinks it's at the root of its own site, paths work as-is, and we proxy directly through the Worker so WebSocket survives.  @NousResearch two flags would seriously help builders here:  • `--public-base-url` so the React bundle prefixes its paths. Would unlock subpath hosting and people could put Hermes anywhere behind any reverse proxy.  • `--managed-mode` to hide the 'Update Hermes' / 'Restart Gateway' buttons when an orchestrator handles lifecycle. Avoids state conflicts.

Recently my daughter had a chronic bout of cough and the doctor suggested giving her a normal saline neb. The medical store guys sent over small vials which I put into the neb without checking, but post the nebulisation I realised it was sterile water and not normal saline. Panick set in, though I am an engineer and I know that logically it’s only steam that she is inhaling and frantically started reaching out to all the Paediatricians I knew. Also no one was responding at that moment. I was quite tensed, and then it clicked me, we have developed the uber for doctors equivalent on https://t.co/ctx4Pu5udt  I fired up our app, hit up the first available Paediatrician I found on the app, and within 5 mins he told me what I wanted to hear, “don’t worry, this is like giving steam”. It cost me about Rs.100 for the 5 mins as the app bills per minute but at that time, I would pay even 2500 to hear those words. I have since started taking follow-ups too via the app and I still haven’t spent Rs.1000 on doctor consultations. I have booked more than 4 times already in the last two days and almost every time, I would have avoided reaching out to my doctors for the asks, but since this was the proposition of the app, I used it without judgement.  Something my boss, @RevantB told me, came to me at this moment. A PM’s job isn’t complete with just conceptualising and building a functionality, but when they communicate and get adoption. I realised I forgot about a feature I developed in the time of what seemed like crisis to me. Therefore I am here communicating about how I leveraged it, and hope helps someone reading in their time of “crisis”. We have the same functionality on “Man Matters” and “Be Bodywise” with a lot more experts. Keep these apps handy, you never know when you may end up needed an instant appointment.

X’s ‘cop’ Nikita Bier ships active user dashboard after weeks of platform cleanup  Nikita Bier, X’s head of product, has announced that a new content optimization feature is now live on the platform, crediting team members Lingyun Gao and Zach Warunek for shipping the update.  The new feature comes after Bier shared screenshots of a conversation from May 18 where a person behind the now-suspended X account, @neptune_btc1, offered to help him boost engagement on X, a clear violation of the platform’s policy.  Bier wrote, “This is equivalent to trying to sell a cop drugs while he’s in uniform in his police car.” The remark captured the part product builder, part platform enforcer posture Nikita has adopted throughout 2026.  Nikita Bier has led X crackdowns in 2026 The “cop” moment was the latest in a string of enforcement actions under Bier’s watch. In January, Cryptopolitan reported that X revoked API access from so-called InfoFi projects, which rewarded users for posting and had flooded the platform with bot-generated replies and AI-written content.  At that time, Bier stated that they “will no longer allow apps that reward users for posting on X (aka “infofi”).  According to Bier, these projects led to an increase in the amount of AI slop and reply spam on the platform, adding that the changes should improve the user experience “once the bots realize they’re not getting paid anymore.”  By April, X had rolled out an auto-lock system targeting accounts that post about cryptocurrency for the first time.  The mechanism forces identity verification before the account can post again, a measure Bier said “should kill 99% of the incentive” for criminals who steal accounts to push fake investment schemes.  The same month, Nikita disclosed that cryptocurrency had become the most muted topic on X since the platform launched its Snooze Topics feature for premium users. Politics, the Iran conflict, and sports followed in the ranking.  How is Bier’s relationship with crypto? While Bier has pushed for crypto integration through features like ticker-linked trading and cashtag labeling, he is also simultaneously cutting off projects and practices he considers parasitic.  In March 2025, Bier announced that he was joining Solana as an advisor, stating that the blockchain had “the fundamental building blocks for something to break out on mobile.”  In the same thread, he acknowledged having “mixed opinions on crypto over the years” but pointed out that the regulatory environment had shifted enough to make the space attractive for consumer product builders.  That advisory role came months before his January crackdown on InfoFi projects and his February comments about crypto spam during X’s. “I genuinely want crypto to proliferate on X,” Bier wrote in February 2026, “but applications that create incentives to spam, raid, and harass random users is not the way.”  What the new feature signals The shipped update, announced with a simple “Shipped” and credits to his team, fits into X’s push toward content optimization tools, including the Creator Connect feature. The feature allows premium users to know the number and percentage of their followers who have been active in the past 24 hours.  Also, X still holds money-handling licenses in more than 40 U.S. states and is still testing its X Money digital wallet. X has over 600 million monthly users and has plans to reach one billion. The platform’s product direction under Bier has combined financial feature expansion with increasingly aggressive content policing.

Everyone’s using Fable 5, but i haven’t seen anything but a website, or one shotted game.  Why isn’t anyone actually pushing its capabilities?   I just built a new feature into my engine, plus shipped it into production.  Here is what every trader gets wrong about news.   The number drops, and they interpret it live, in the moment, with money on the line. That first candle lies constantly. They get faked out, or they hold a bias from a number the market already ignored.  My engine does not interpret in the moment. It writes the playbook before the print.   -Event appears → engine pre-builds every branch. Hot, inline, cool. What each one pressures, what each one relieves.   -It already knows the confirm conditions: hot CPI is only real if the dollar is up, yields are up, and the index breaks lower. One candle is not confirmation. Four markets agreeing is.  -Number drops → reality gets graded against the playbook on the release candle, live.  -Price confirms a break of flow → the event is promoted to a driver and the engine keeps monitoring.   -Market shrugs → marked ignored. Knowing the news didn’t matter is an edge too.  -Effect dies → it fades off the read   Then it hands you one sentence. 'CPI came in hot with the read, toward the working objective.' Not a red icon on a calendar. A verdict.  Why have Fable and not push its limits?

So far, we're loving the integration with @xStocksFi .  There are three things that stand out to me above all else.   1/ Undoubtedly the first thing that comes to mind is the power of DeFi integrations. On @solana, our home chain, we're plugged into @Raydium and @kamino - which provide out of the box support with liquidity pools for our 'market-making' strategy (risk-managed concentrated liquidity pools) and  lending, respectively. Alongside our own platform's trust and security, we believe users will be reassured that the assets are backed by @krakenfx, a global and well regarded exchange.   3/ Primary liquidity, when users need it, is the lifeblood of any RWA. Big changes in about 48 days. Here’s the protocol I followed:  1. Lifting 3x per week. Only 3 working sets per exercise. Lift heavy and go to true failure.  2. Acrobatics/Gymnastics 3x per week. Lots of handstand skill work. Long sessions with lots of rest. I typically don’t go to fatigue for skill training.  3. 140mg Testosterone weekly. At age 43, it’s a major boost.  4. Low dose Tirzepitide - 2.5mg only, and do not increase dose to create dependency - as suggested by @gregogallagher   5. NAD+ for mitochondrial health  6. Diet is heavy protein and minimal/zero processed sugar. Still eating plenty of carbs in the form of rice.  7. My sleep is still not great, as that has just been an on-going thing for years. Still working on it though.  8. Water. Lots of water. I have a SodaStream so it’s not as boring as just pounding regular water all day.  9. 10K steps has become the floor. Using my Oura Ring for general tracking, but I’m so used to going on multiple short walks for 20 minutes each day that I pretty much stopped checking it.  10. Purpose. In the midst of building out The Handstand School’s site and course offerings that each day I’m hyper focused on growing our platform, not sitting around bored and wondering what to do with my life.  This all has worked well for me personally, and might not be aligned with the goals you are going for. I’m not trying to get huge or anything, as hand balancing is much easier when you are lighter and carrying less weight :)
